---
title: "Glossary & Metrics"
---

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary

A reference for every term, metric, acronym, and project-specific
convention used elsewhere in this report. Organised by category so a
reader who hits an unfamiliar term in Section 6 can come here, find
the definition, and return to where they were. Entries are
deliberately short — one to three sentences each — and prefer
plainness over completeness.
:::

---

## Evaluation Metrics

The numbers reported in Section 6 are produced by `eval/scorer.py`
and aggregated by `eval/report.py`. Every metric below appears in
the canonical `report.md` at
`eval/results/2026-06-18T15-32-07Z/`.

**`citation_recall`** — For a single answerable question, the
fraction of gold chunk identifiers that appear at least once in
the model's emitted citations. Reported as the mean across all
questions in a subset. Range 0.0 to 1.0; higher is better. The
load-bearing metric for the project's headline claim.

**`citation_precision`** — For a single answerable question, the
fraction of the model's emitted citations that match a gold chunk
identifier. Reported as the mean across all questions in a subset.
Range 0.0 to 1.0; higher is better. A model that cites every
retrieved chunk regardless of relevance would have high recall and
low precision.

**`citation_f1`** — Per-question harmonic mean of precision and
recall, then averaged across the subset. Range 0.0 to 1.0; higher
is better. Useful when a single summary statistic is needed but
the recall/precision split is more diagnostic for tuning work.

**`refusal correctness`** — For a refusal-category question,
Boolean: `True` if the model emitted the exact refusal phrase
("I cannot answer this from the provided sources.") and nothing
else; `False` otherwise. Reported as `correct/total` per cell.

**`hallucinations`** — Absolute count, across an entire cell's
44-question answerable sweep, of citations whose `chunk_id` did
not appear in the retrieved context. Lower is better. Zero is the
hallucination floor; the production-default cell achieves it.

**`retrieval_recall`** — Per-question fraction of gold chunks
that appear in the retriever's top-k output, computed independently
of the language model's behaviour. A question with
`retrieval_recall = 0` is the diagnostic signal for an upstream
retrieval miss (Q12). Not always reported alongside `citation_recall`
but used internally to compute the `_retrievable` subsets below.

**`latency_ans` / `latency_ref`** — Mean wall-clock per question,
in seconds, measured at `AnswerResult.elapsed_seconds`. Covers
retrieve + generate. `latency_ans` is over the answerable subset;
`latency_ref` is over the refusal subset. Refusal latency is
typically lower because the refusal phrase is shorter than a
citation-rich answer.

**`citation_f1` vs `citation_recall` — which to read** — For
within-subset comparisons across models, `citation_recall` is the
load-bearing axis. For absolute system quality, `citation_f1` is a
more honest summary. The headline tables in Section 6 report both.

---

## Subset Definitions

Each subset in Section 6's subset-analysis table is a strict
partition of the 44 answerable questions according to a stated
property. Subset definitions live in `eval/scorer.py` and are
verified at sweep-startup time.

| Subset | Definition |
|---|---|
| `all_answerable` | All 44 answerable questions, regardless of retrieval outcome |
| `excluding_retrieval_misses` | The 33 answerable questions where at least one cell of the sweep retrieved at least one gold chunk |
| `single_chunk` | The 22 answerable questions where the benchmark designates exactly one gold chunk |
| `single_chunk_retrievable` | The 17 questions in `single_chunk` where retrieval succeeded in at least one cell |
| `multi_chunk` | The 12 answerable questions where the benchmark designates multiple gold chunks in the same document |
| `within_document` | The 34 answerable questions whose gold chunks all come from a single document (`single_chunk` ∪ `multi_chunk`) |
| `within_document_retrievable` | The 27 questions in `within_document` where retrieval succeeded |
| `cross_document` | The 10 answerable questions whose gold chunks span two issuers; the system's clearest weakness subset |

: Subset partitions used in Section 6's subset-analysis table.
{#tbl-glossary-subsets}

The `_retrievable` qualifier is the key one for reading the table.
A `_retrievable` subset isolates *answer-stage* quality from the
*retrieval-stage* bottleneck described in Section 8. The ratio of
`all_answerable` to `excluding_retrieval_misses` is the size of
that bottleneck.

---

## Refusal Categories

The refusal benchmark partitions 26 questions across three
adversarial categories of increasing subtlety. The categories are
defined in `eval/benchmark.toml` and reported separately in
Section 6's refusal-correctness table.

**`refusal_out_of_corpus`** (n=10) — Questions whose topic is
clearly outside the corpus's domain. The system is expected to
refuse without ambiguity. "What is the maximum solvency capital
ratio in Bermuda?" is an example shape — the corpus covers UK and
EU regulation only.

**`refusal_adjacent`** (n=10) — Questions whose topic the corpus
discusses qualitatively but whose specific demand the corpus does
not contain. This is the most operationally important refusal
category: a model is most tempted to confabulate when the topic is
in scope but the specific demand is not. "What is Swiss Re's
exact revenue-percentage threshold for thermal coal exclusion?"
is an example shape.

**`refusal_false_premise`** (n=6) — Questions that assume a fact
not in the corpus. Even if the model attempted to answer in good
faith, the answer would be based on a fabricated premise. "Why
did Munich Re withdraw from the Net-Zero Insurance Alliance in
2024?" is an example shape — the corpus does not contain that
event.

---

## Reinsurance and Regulatory Terminology

For readers without an insurance background. Defined as used in
the corpus and in this report, not in maximum generality.

**Reinsurer** — A company that insures other insurance companies
("cedants") against catastrophic, aggregated, or specialised
risks the cedant cannot absorb alone. Munich Re and Swiss Re are
the two largest globally.

**Cedant** — An insurance company that transfers ("cedes") part
of its risk portfolio to a reinsurer. The project's codename
("Cedant") references this term.

**Underwriter** — The reinsurance professional who assesses,
prices, and accepts or declines individual risks. Most of the
research work the system supports is what an underwriter would
otherwise do by hand.

**Treaty reinsurance** — A standing agreement covering an entire
class of risks the cedant writes; renegotiated annually.
Contrasted with **facultative reinsurance**, which covers
individual risks one at a time.

**PRA** — Prudential Regulation Authority. The UK insurance and
banking supervisor. Publishes Supervisory Statements (SS)
numbered by year (`SS5/25` is statement 5 of 2025).

**EIOPA** — European Insurance and Occupational Pensions
Authority. The EU-level insurance supervisor under Solvency II.
Publishes Guidelines and Recommendations.

**Solvency II** — The EU regulatory framework for insurance
capital adequacy, governance, and disclosure, in force since
2016. Sets the structural shape of much EU-jurisdiction
underwriting documentation.

**Supervisory Statement (SS)** — A PRA publication setting out
the regulator's expectations for how firms should approach a
specific topic. `SS5/25` (climate-related risks, 2025) and
`SS1/21` (operational resilience, 2021) are in the indexed
corpus.

**ORSA** — Own Risk and Solvency Assessment. An EU-required
internal risk and capital assessment that insurers must conduct
at least annually. Heavily referenced in EIOPA's System of
Governance guidelines.

**Sustainability report** — An annual disclosure by a listed
reinsurer covering ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)
positions: climate exposure, underwriting exclusions, board
oversight, and investment policy. Munich Re 2023 and Swiss Re
2024 are in the indexed corpus.

**Thermal coal exclusion** — A common underwriting policy
position adopted by major reinsurers under climate pressure,
restricting or refusing coverage for new thermal coal mining or
coal-fired power generation projects. The benchmark uses Munich
Re's and Swiss Re's positions on this as a canonical
cross-document synthesis example.

---

## Retrieval Terminology

**RAG** — Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The general pattern of
combining a retriever (which finds relevant chunks from a
corpus) with a language model (which generates an answer using
those chunks). Cedant is a cited-RAG system.

**Chunk** — A small piece of a source document — typically 100
to 1,500 tokens of text, with metadata identifying its parent
document and section path. The unit of retrieval and citation.

**`chunk_id`** — The stable string identifier for a chunk.
Combines the source `document_id`, a four-digit sequence number,
and a section slug
(e.g. `pra_ss5-25_climate__0027__role-of-scenario-analysis`).
The unit of citation in the model's output.

**Dense retrieval** — Retrieval based on vector similarity between
the query's embedding and each chunk's embedding. Captures
paraphrase and semantic similarity. Good at conceptual queries;
weaker on rare named entities.

**Sparse retrieval** — Retrieval based on token-overlap statistics
between the query and each chunk. The standard algorithm is BM25.
Good at exact-match queries on named entities; weaker on
paraphrase.

**Hybrid retrieval** — Running both dense and sparse retrieval in
parallel and combining their results. The standard combination
strategy is Reciprocal Rank Fusion. The basis of Cedant's
retrieval layer per D009.

**Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** — Standard rank-based fusion of
two retrieval rankings. For each document appearing in either
channel, the fused score is $\sum_c 1/(k + r_c)$ where $r_c$ is
the document's 1-based rank in channel $c$ and $k$ is a fusion
constant (60 in Cedant). Sidesteps score-normalisation between
channels.

**`top_k`** — The number of chunks the retriever returns to the
language model for a single query. Cedant defaults to 5;
configurable per call.

**`candidates_per_channel`** — The number of chunks each
retrieval channel surfaces before fusion. Cedant defaults to 50;
fusion is applied to the combined candidate pool.

**BGE-M3** — A multilingual embedding model from BAAI
(`BAAI/bge-m3`) producing 1024-dimensional dense embeddings. Used
in Cedant via `mlx-embeddings` for Apple Silicon acceleration.

**BM25** — Best Matching 25, the standard statistical retrieval
ranking algorithm. Cedant uses default parameters `k1=1.5`,
`b=0.75` (per D011) over a custom Porter-stemmed vocabulary of
~4,810 terms.

**CLS pooling** — Using the model's special classification-token
output as the embedding of an entire input sequence. The
alternative is mean-pooling across all tokens. BGE-M3 was trained
for CLS pooling; Cedant uses it per D010.

**L2 normalisation** — Scaling an embedding vector so its
Euclidean norm equals 1. Makes cosine similarity equivalent to
dot product. Cedant L2-normalises every dense embedding before
indexing or querying.

**Porter stemming** — A rule-based suffix-stripping algorithm
that maps inflected English words to a shared root form
("regulation" / "regulations" / "regulatory" → "regul"). Used in
Cedant's BM25 tokeniser per D011.

**Qdrant** — An open-source vector database supporting both
dense and sparse vector storage and a hybrid query API. Cedant
uses Qdrant in local filesystem-backed mode at
`scratch/qdrant/`.

**Query/chunk language asymmetry** — The phenomenon, diagnosed
in Q12, where a query phrased one way (e.g. as a meta-policy
question) and a chunk written another way (e.g. as a specific
operational statement) embed into different neighbourhoods of
the embedding space even when they share underlying topic. The
root cause of Cedant's 25% retrieval miss rate.

**HyDE** — Hypothetical Document Embeddings. A query-rewriting
technique that asks the language model to draft a hypothetical
answer to the query, then embeds that draft for retrieval instead
of the original query. One of the three v2 remediation paths
proposed in Q13.

**Cross-encoder reranker** — A second-stage retrieval pass that
re-ranks a wide candidate set (e.g. top-50) using a model that
processes the query and each candidate jointly. Generally
higher-quality than the bi-encoder retrieval used in the first
stage, but slower per candidate. One of the three v2 paths in
Q13.

**ColBERT** — A late-interaction retrieval architecture that
produces multiple vectors per document and computes similarity
across the matrix at query time. Stronger than single-vector
retrieval on paraphrase-asymmetric queries; not implemented in
v1 because it requires switching off `mlx-embeddings` (Q7).

---

## Generation and LLM Terminology

**System prompt** — The instruction text the language model
receives before the user's question. Defines the model's role,
the citation format, and the refusal rule. Cedant's frozen
production prompt is `SYSTEM_PROMPT` in
`src/underwriting_copilot/answer.py`.

**Temperature** — A parameter controlling the randomness of the
language model's output. Cedant hard-pins temperature to 0.0 for
determinism: same query plus same model plus same prompt produces
the same answer.

**oMLX** — A local serving framework providing an OpenAI-
compatible chat-completions API for MLX-quantised models on
Apple Silicon. Cedant uses oMLX at `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1`.

**MLX** — Apple's open-source array and machine-learning
framework optimised for Apple Silicon (M-series chips). The
substrate for Cedant's local-first generation stack.

**Quantisation** — Reducing the numeric precision of a model's
weights (e.g. from 16-bit float to 4-bit or 6-bit integer) to
trade a small quality loss for a large reduction in memory and
latency. Cedant uses Gemma 4 31B IT at 6-bit MLX quantisation
and Qwen3.6 35B-A3B at 4-bit.

**LLM-as-judge** — Using a separate language model to score the
output of another model. Cedant does **not** use LLM-as-judge in
the v1 eval; scoring is structural. LLM-as-judge semantic
correctness is recorded as Q10.3 and deferred to v2.

---

## Citation and Refusal Contract Terminology

**Citation contract** — The system-level commitment that every
factual claim in an answer will be followed by a `[chunk_id]`
referring to a chunk in the retrieved context, validated
structurally by set-membership at parse time.

**Citation format** — The specific syntactic form
(`[chunk_id]`) used to mark citations. Defined by the system
prompt; enforced by the parser regex.

**`CITATION_REGEX`** — The compiled regular expression
(`\[([A-Za-z0-9_\-]+)\]`) that extracts every citation from the
model's output. Strict character class rejects spaces and
punctuation; natural-language brackets in regulatory text are
not matched.

**Valid citation** — A citation whose `chunk_id` is in the set
of chunks the retriever surfaced to the language model.

**Hallucinated citation** — A citation whose `chunk_id` is not
in the retrieved set. Partitioned out of the `citations` field of
`AnswerResult` and surfaced separately as
`hallucinated_citations`. Rendered as red `[?]` badges in the
Streamlit UI.

**Refusal contract** — The system-level commitment that the
language model will emit the exact phrase *"I cannot answer
this from the provided sources."* (and nothing else) when the
retrieved chunks do not contain the answer.

**Refusal detector** — The strict-string-match function that
checks whether the model's output matches the refusal phrase,
after stripping trailing whitespace and `.!?` punctuation.

**Partial refusal** — An output that contains the refusal phrase
as a clause but also includes other content. Rejected by the
detector; counted as a non-refusal in the eval. None occurred
on the N=70 sweep.

---

## Methodology and Discipline Terminology

**D-number / Decision** — A numbered architectural decision
recorded in `docs/decisions.md` with date, status, context,
decision, and trade-offs. D001 through D015 are lodged for v1.
Append-only; superseded decisions are marked but not deleted.

**Q-number / Open Question** — A numbered open research or
design question recorded in `docs/open_questions.md`. Each
question resolves into a D-entry, a deferral, or a closure.
Examples cited in this report: Q7 (BGE-M3 multi-vector), Q10
(GEPA/DSPy), Q11 (production model), Q12 (retrieval miss
diagnostic, closed), Q13 (retrieval remediation, v2 work-stream).

**Falsification criterion** — A pre-stated rule specifying what
evidence would force a claim's retraction or weakening. Cedant
uses falsification criteria for the D014 sweep design (Section
5). Stating the criterion before running the experiment removes
post-hoc rationalisation as a coping strategy.

**Pre-stated** — Recorded in `docs/decisions.md` before the
experiment whose evidence would test the claim. Distinguished
from post-stated criteria, which are written after the data is
seen.

**Retraction** — A formal withdrawal of an earlier claim,
recorded in `docs/journal.md` by timestamp and commit hash,
append-only. The original claim is not edited; the retraction
is appended. Cedant has two retractions on record: the Day-2
family-axis claim and the N=40 within-document parity claim.

**Weakening** — A partial retraction: the qualitative finding
survives but the quantitative claim does not. The N=40 →
N=70 within-document parity claim is a weakening, not a full
retraction.

**Probe** — A cheap, focused experiment designed to falsify a
specific hypothesis quickly before committing to a larger
investigation. The Q12 diagnostic ran three probes (top_k
widening, RRF parameter sweep, dense-channel localisation), each
cheaper than the next.

**Append-only** — A documentation discipline where history is
preserved verbatim and updates are appended rather than
edited-over. `docs/decisions.md`, `docs/journal.md`, and
`docs/open_questions.md` are append-only by convention; state
documents (`docs/status.md`, `docs/architecture.md`) are
overwritten freely.

**Sweep cell** — A single (model, prompt, question) tuple in the
evaluation grid. The N=70 D014 sweep is 2 models × 2 prompts × 70
questions = 280 cells.

**Wall-clock** — Elapsed real time, as opposed to CPU time. All
latency numbers in Section 6 are wall-clock measured at
`AnswerResult.elapsed_seconds`.

---

## Conventions in This Report

**Bold numbers in cell tables** indicate the production-default
cell (`gemma-4-31B-it-MLX-6bit × v2`) per D015. Used consistently
in Sections 6 and 9.

**`monospace` formatting** is used for code identifiers (Python
class and function names), CLI invocations, file paths, and
configuration values that appear verbatim in source code or
configuration files.

**Italics** are used for direct quotations from the system
prompt, the refusal phrase, and definitional emphasis on first
use of a term.

**Cross-references to documentation** — paths like
`docs/decisions.md` or `docs/journal.md` refer to files in the
repository root's `docs/` directory, not to sections of this
report. Section references within this report use
`@sec-NAME-ID` (Quarto cross-reference syntax) where applicable.

**N=40 vs N=70** — Two canonical evaluation sweeps were
committed: the original D014 sweep (N=40) and the extended
follow-up (N=70). Numbers in this report are from N=70 unless
otherwise stated; Section 5 narrates both.

**v1 vs v2** — When unqualified, refers to the system prompt
version (the two prompts in the D014 sweep). `v1` is the
original (with the `[chunk_id]` literal placeholder ambiguity);
`v2` is the revised production prompt (with `<ID>` metasyntax
and one worked example). When qualified with "version" or
"release", refers to the project version: v1 is the artefact
described in this report; v2 is the proposed next phase.
